source_title: Distinct Morphological Stages of Dentate Granule Neuron Maturation in the Adult Mouse Hippocampus
node_relevance_reason: Directly defines the four morphological maturation stages (A-D) of adult-born dentate granule neurons in mouse, with precise day-post-infection (dpi) milestones for axon extension, dendritic growth, and spine formation — the primary morphological timeline for dg_immature_granule_neuron node.
summary: Zhao 2006 used retroviral GFP labelling in adult mice to track newborn dentate granule neurons through four morphological stages (A-D). Stage A involves polarization, migration, and initial neurite outgrowth. Stage B sees further axonal (reaching CA3 at 10-11 dpi) and dendritic growth (outer molecular layer by ~21 dpi). Stage C begins at ~16 dpi with spine formation, when major synaptic connections form. Stage D involves ongoing mushroom-spine remodelling lasting months. Spine density rises from 0.43 to 2.61 spines/µm between 21-56 dpi. At 28 dpi, 71.7% of GFP+ cells co-express NeuN. The study is conducted in adult mouse (mus musculus).
quotes: Axonal projections of newborn neurons reach the CA3 area 10–11 d after viral infection, 5–6 d before the first spines are formed.
spine formation starts at ∼16 dpi in granule neurons born in the adult mouse brain.
spine density changed from 0.43 ± 0.09 to 1.95 ± 0.08 spines per micrometer
Spine density continued to increase until 56 dpi (2.61 ± 0.07 spines per micrometer; 56 dpi) when it reached a plateau.

Stage B is the stage of further axonal and dendritic growth that sets up the basic organization for synaptic connections. During stage B, dendritic processes reach the outer edge of the molecular layer, and axon fibers reach the CA3 area.
Stage C is when the major synaptic connections are formed.
71.7 ± 8.2% of GFP+ cells were of neuronal phenotype, which was determined by the coexpression of the neuronal marker NeuN at 28 dpi.

source_title: Distinct Morphological Stages of Dentate Granule Neuron Maturation in the Adult Mouse Hippocampus
node_relevance_reason: Provides precise dendritic arborization timeline for dg_immature_granule_neuron morphology field: inner molecular layer by 10 dpi, middle molecular layer by 14 dpi, outer molecular layer by 21 dpi — directly supporting the node's axon/dendrite timing data.
summary: Zhao 2006 tracks dendritic growth of adult-born granule neurons in mouse DG using retroviral GFP labelling. Apical dendrites reach the inner molecular layer at 10 dpi, middle molecular layer at ~14 dpi, and edge of molecular layer by some neurons at 21 dpi. This progression represents the immature granule neuron stage when neurons are postmitotic but not yet fully synaptically integrated. Comparison to postnatal mice shows spine growth initiation is delayed by ~4 days in adult-born neurons.
quotes: The apical dendrites of GFP+ neurons reached the inner molecular layer at 10 dpi, and their arborization appeared more elaborate than that at 7 dpi.
The dendritic processes grew to the middle of the molecular layer at ∼14 dpi and reached the edge of the molecular layer in some of the neurons at 21 dpi.
the initiation of spine growth in newborn granule neurons was delayed for ∼4 d in the adult mouse brain.

source_title: Survey of transcriptome analyses of hippocampal neurogenesis with focus on adult dentate gyrus stem cells
node_relevance_reason: Directly describes the sequential marker progression through neuroblast stages in adult DG neurogenesis (type-2a Nestin+/Sox2+; type-2b Nestin+/DCX+; type-3 DCX+; stage-5 DCX+/NeuN+; stage-6 Calbindin+/NeuN+), exactly matching the node context classification system.
summary: This survey paper provides a clear sequential description of marker progression from neural stem cell through neuroblast stages (type-2a Nestin+/Sox2+, type-2b Nestin+/DCX+, type-3 DCX+) to immature postmitotic granule neurons (stage 5: DCX+/NeuN+) and terminally differentiated neurons (stage 6: Calbindin+/NeuN+). It also notes that immature dentate granule cells (imGCs) are functionally different from mature neurons and that primates have a higher proportion of imGCs than rodents.
quotes: Neural stem cells (NSCs) progressively develop into proliferating neural progenitor cells (NPCs), designated as type-2a (Nestin+/Sox2+), type-2b cells (expressing Nestin and doublecortin: Nestin+/DCX+) and neuroblasts (type-3, DCX+). Neuroblasts progress toward immature postmitotic granule neurons co-expressing DCX and NeuN (stage 5), and eventually become terminally differentiated neurons (stage 6) expressing calbindin and NeuN.
Increasing evidence in mouse is attributing a role in the adult hippocampal plasticity to imGCs, which are functionally different from mature neurons.
The primate hippocampus has a longer maturation period of newly generated granule cells (GCs) and a higher percentage of immature dentate GCs, compared with rodents.

source_title: Tis21 Expression Marks Not Only Populations of Neurogenic Precursor Cells but Also New Postmitotic Neurons in Adult Hippocampal Neurogenesis
node_relevance_reason: Directly characterizes Tis21 as a marker of neurogenic precursors AND postmitotic neurons in adult DG; establishes co-expression relationships between Tis21, DCX, NeuN, and Calbindin at different maturation stages — directly relevant to dg_neuroblast and dg_immature_granule_neuron marker panels.
summary: Attardo 2009 established that Tis21-GFP marks both proliferating neurogenic precursors (type-2 cells) and postmitotic neurons in adult dentate gyrus. Tis21 co-localizes with DCX in early neuroblast stages and then with NeuN and Calbindin in mature stages. Calretinin (CR) shows minimal overlap with Tis21-GFP, distinguishing it from Tis21 as an early postmitotic marker. The study uses BrdU pulse-chase to show temporal progression of marker co-expression.
quotes: Concomitant with this, an increasing proportion of the BrdU/Tis21-GFP–double-positive cells expressed markers of postmitotic neurons, that is, NeuN and CB
The number of Dcx/Tis21-GFP–double-positive cells decreased with the reduction of the Dcx cell population
In contrast to the fetal brain, where postmitotic neurons do not express Tis21...in the postnatal and adult DG, Tis21 (as revealed by Tis21-GFP) is also expressed in postmitotic neurons.

source_title: Intermediate Progenitors in Adult Hippocampal Neurogenesis: Tbr2 Expression and Coordinate Regulation of Neuronal Output
section: Characterization of Tbr2+ cells in the SGZ
node_relevance_reason: Directly characterizes Tbr2 (Eomes) as a marker of intermediate progenitor cells (type-2) in the adult DG SGZ, showing co-expression with Nestin and DCX. Establishes the transition from Tbr2+ IPC to DCX+ neuroblast to Tbr1+ mature granule cell — filling a key gap in the dg_neuroblast node regarding type-2a/2b subtype markers.
summary: Hodge 2008 demonstrates that Tbr2 marks intermediate progenitor cells (IPCs) in the adult SGZ, predominantly co-localizing with Nestin-GFP (96.76%) and DCX (64.4%). Tbr2+ cells expressing DCX have low DCX expression and short processes consistent with type-2 progenitors. Tbr1 marks only postmitotic granule cells. Type-3 IPCs generate immature neurons that integrate as mature granule cells within 1 month.
quotes: Tbr2+ cells coexpressing nestin-GFP were more typically found in clusters in the SGZ and were often noted to be in close association with a Tbr2-negative type-1 nestin-GFP+ cell. Of all the nestin-GFP+, Tbr2+ double-labeled cells examined, 96.76 ± 0.39% had morphology consistent with type-2 progenitors

The majority of Tbr2+ cells colabeled with DCX (64.4 ± 4.7%). In general, these cells had low DCX expression, with either no processes or short, tangentially oriented processes, typical of type-2 cells.
Tbr1 expression was observed only in postmitotic granule cells and was not detected in progenitor cells, because Tbr1 never colocalized with nestin-GFP.

source_title: Neurogenesis in neurodegenerative diseases in the adult human brain
node_relevance_reason: Provides a concise description of the three cell types (type I/II/neuroblast/immature neuron) in DG granule layer neurogenesis with their associated markers (GFAP, Sox2, Nestin, DCX, Ki67, PSA-NCAM, NeuN), directly supporting the marker panels for dg_neuroblast and dg_immature_granule_neuron nodes.
summary: Stepien 2021 describes neurogenesis in the DG granular layer using a clear cell-type classification: type I (GFAP+/Sox2+), type II (Nestin+), neuroblasts (DCX+/Ki67+), and immature neurons (DCX+/PSA-NCAM+/NeuN+). This directly maps onto the node context definitions and confirms the co-expression of DCX and NeuN as a key feature of the immature granule neuron stage.
quotes: Three types of proliferatively active cells have been identified in the granular layer of the dentate gyrus (DG) of the hippocampus: type I cells – radial glial-like stem cells expressing glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP) and Sox2; type II cells – non-sessile cells expressing nestin, also referred to as transiently activated progenitor cells, neuroblasts expressing doublecortin (DCX); and Ki67 proteins and immature neurons expressing the DCX protein, PSA-NCAM, a marker of migrating neurons (polysialylated neuronal cell adhesion molecules) and neuron-specific protein (NeuN).

source_title: Adult neurogenesis: a real hope or a delusion?
node_relevance_reason: Directly addresses electrophysiological maturation of newborn neurons in the dentate gyrus — the most critical gap identified in the node context (dg_neuroblast ephys GAP, dg_immature_granule_neuron ephys GAP). Specifically states that intrinsic electrical properties develop alongside synaptic and morphological properties.
summary: Hussain 2023 describes the neurogenic process in the hippocampus from NSC through granule cell, noting that synaptic integration, intrinsic electrical characteristics, and neuronal morphology all develop concurrently. The paper also establishes the trajectory from IPCs through neuroblasts to granule neurons that integrate into hippocampal circuitry and sustain memory function.
quotes: NSCs in the hippocampus give rise to GCs through a regulated process that includes emergence from a quiescence state, posterior divisions, specification to a neuronal fate, neuronal differentiation, and physiological integration in the pre-existing hippocampal circuits. Synapses, intrinsic electrical characteristics, and neuronal morphology all develop concurrently throughout this time toward a fully developed neuronal phenotype.

In DG, RGL cells produce IPCs that develop into neuroblasts and produce granule neurons. As granule neurons differentiate, integrate into hippocampal circuitry, and sustain hippocampus-dependent memory function, their migration is constrained.

source_title: Evidence that Doublecortin Is Dispensable for the Development of Adult Born Neurons in Mice
node_relevance_reason: Directly addresses DCX as the canonical marker for immature newborn neurons in DG and SVZ, and demonstrates the maturation transition through calretinin (immature) and NeuN (mature) marker expression. Confirms dg_neuroblast and dg_immature_granule_neuron marker profiles.
summary: Merz 2013 establishes that DCX is almost exclusively expressed by immature newborn neurons in the DG and SVZ/OB-system and is used to distinguish immature neurons from non-neuronally committed precursors and mature neurons. Calretinin is expressed in immature dentate granule neurons (distinct from mature Calbindin+ stage), and NeuN marks mature neurons. DCX overexpression or knockdown does not accelerate the transition to NeuN-positive stage.
quotes: DCX is almost exclusively expressed by immature newborn neurons in the DG and the SVZ/OB-system and is commonly used to distinguish immature neurons from non-neuronally committed precursors and mature neurons, and to estimate neurogenic activity.

Brain sections were stained for calretinin, a calcium binding protein, which is expressed in immature dentate granule neurons. 11.29% ±2.13% of the RFP control cells were positive for calretinin whereas 8.05% ±1.32% of DCX overexpressing green cells were calretinin positive.

At 10 dpi, 80% of the control cells were already NeuN positive... DCX knockdown did not significantly enhance the fraction of NeuN-expressing cells, demonstrating that loss-of-DCX does not promote the transition to a NeuN positive developmental stage.

source_title: Advances toward regenerative medicine in the central nervous system: challenges in making stem cell therapy a viable clinical strategy
section: Problems [and emerging solutions] for stem cell transplants within the central nervous system
node_relevance_reason: Contains a direct statement about immature neuron electrophysiology — that immature neurons are highly excitable and affect network dynamics — addressing the dg_neuroblast and dg_immature_granule_neuron electrophysiology gaps. Also describes neuroprogenitor marker cascade (GFAP+/Nestin+/Dcx− → GFAP−/Nestin+/Dcx+ → lineage-restricted neuronal precursors).
summary: Stoll 2014 includes a statement that immature neurons are highly excitable and affect network properties, providing partial electrophysiology characterization relevant to the identified gaps. It also describes the adult DG as maintaining a tertiary proliferative matrix established by P5, with cells proliferating in the inner GCL and migrating radially. Progenitor marker cascade is described: quiescent GFAP+/Nestin+/Dcx− (B-type) → GFAP−/Nestin+/Dcx+ (C-type) → lineage-restricted transit-amplifying neuronal precursors (A-type).
quotes: Immature neurons are highly excitable, so newly-born cells are thought to affect the dynamic properties of existing networks to which they are recruited.

These largely quiescent, GFAP + Nestin + Dcx- stem-like cells (called B-type) intercalate into the ependymal layer as they divide. These divisions produce a highly proliferative GFAP-Nestin + Dcx + progenitor cell (called C-type); this progenitor in turn yields two or more lineage-restricted, transit-amplifying, neuronal precursors (called A-type).

source_title: Enhanced synaptic plasticity in newly generated granule cells of the adult hippocampus
node_relevance_reason: Primary electrophysiology paper for dg_immature_granule_neuron: demonstrates that young adult-born granule cells have distinct active and passive membrane properties compared to mature granule cells, including T-type Ca2+ channels enabling Ca2+ spikes, and a lower threshold for LTP induction — directly filling the electrophysiology gap in the dg_immature_granule_neuron and dg_mature_granule_neuron nodes.
summary: Schmidt-Hieber et al. 2004 show in adult mouse hippocampus that newly generated granule cells have substantially different membrane properties from mature cells, express T-type Ca2+ channels capable of generating isolated Ca2+ spikes and amplifying Na+ action potentials, and exhibit a lower threshold for associative LTP induction. These unique mechanisms are proposed to facilitate new memory formation by providing immature neurons with enhanced synaptic plasticity. No neuropeptide data are reported. Species: mouse.
quotes: young granule cells in the adult hippocampus have substantially different active and passive membrane properties from mature cells
Young neurons express T-type Ca2+ channels, which are able to generate isolated calcium spikes and to amplify fast sodium action potentials
Associative long-term potentiation can be induced more easily in young neurons than in mature neurons under identical conditions
newly generated neurons express unique mechanisms to facilitate synaptic plasticity, which may be important for the formation of new memories

source_title: Neuronal differentiation in the adult hippocampus recapitulates embryonic development
node_relevance_reason: Provides comprehensive patch-clamp characterisation of the full maturation sequence from silent neuron through GABA-only to GABA+glutamate stages in adult mouse DG, with precise dpi milestones and membrane property values (Vrest, Rin, Cm) at each stage — directly filling the electrophysiology gap for dg_neuroblast and dg_immature_granule_neuron nodes. Documents depolarised EGABA of GABAergic inputs in immature neurons.
summary: Espósito et al. 2005 used retroviral GFP labelling in adult female C57BL/6 mice to track newly born dentate granule cells through whole-cell patch-clamp recordings. Neurons progress through a stereotyped sequence: silent (1–7 dpi, high Rin 4.3 GΩ, low Cm 6.4 pF, Vrest −50.6 mV) → slow GABAergic inputs only (8–18 dpi, Vrest −45.6 mV) → glutamatergic inputs added (≥18 dpi, Vrest −63.2 mV, Cm 24.6 pF) → fast perisomatic GABAergic inputs last. GABAergic PSCs show a depolarised reversal potential (EGABA = −26.9 mV), indicating immature chloride homeostasis. This sequence mirrors embryonic hippocampal development, suggesting cell-autonomous programming. No neuropeptides reported.
quotes: All GFP+ neurons studied between 1 and 7 dpi were silent, and their morphology seemed to correspond to class A or B.
Neurons with GABAergic but not glutamatergic afferents were first observed at 8 dpi.
Glutamatergic PSCs were detected in GFP+ neurons bearing GABAergic inputs from 18 dpi onwards.
The development of afferent synaptic connections takes ∼4 weeks and follows the sequence: silent → slow GABA → glu → fast GABA.

During the first week neurons had an irregular shape and immature spikes and were synaptically silent. Slow GABAergic synaptic inputs first appeared during the second week...In contrast, glutamatergic afferents were detected by the fourth week in neurons displaying mature excitability and morphology. Interestingly, fast GABAergic responses were the latest to appear.
GABAergic-evoked PSCs displayed a reversal potential (EGABA) of -26.9 ± 1.9 mV.
Silent neurons: Vrest (mV): − 50.6 ± 2.3; Cm (pF): 6.4 ± 0.4; Rinput (MΩ): 4.3 ± 0.5
GABA neurons: Vrest (mV): − 45.6 ± 2.3; Cm (pF): 11.7 ± 1.2; Rinput (MΩ): 2.9 ± 0.4
GABA and glu neurons: Vrest (mV): − 63.2 ± 2.8; Cm (pF): 24.6 ± 1.4; Rinput (MΩ): 0.62 ± 0.10
It is striking that the sequence of events observed in the adult DG is also followed during hippocampal development.

The finding that the developmental sequence for neuronal maturation in the developing and adult brain is preserved in environments that are so distinct suggests that such sequence is primarily determined by a cell-autonomous program rather than by environmental cues.

source_title: Neuronal differentiation in the adult hippocampus recapitulates embryonic development
node_relevance_reason: Characterises GABA synaptic current kinetics at the immature granule neuron stage: slow dendritic currents precede fast perisomatic currents, with distinct EGABA values for each component, demonstrating a somatic-to-dendritic chloride gradient. Critical for filling the dg_immature_granule_neuron electrophysiology gap regarding GABAergic input maturation.
summary: Espósito et al. 2005 describes two GABA PSC components in immature adult-born dentate granule cells: a slow dendritic component (EGABA −44.5 ± 2.1 mV) and a fast perisomatic component (EGABA −30.9 ± 1.6 mV). The depolarised EGABA of both components reflects elevated intracellular Cl− in immature neurons. The fast perisomatic component is selectively blocked by somatic BMI application, while the slow dendritic component requires bath application. Fast GABAergic perisomatic inputs are the final synaptic inputs acquired during maturation. Species: adult female C57BL/6 mouse.
quotes: Current-voltage curves revealed two components with similar conductance (Gmax): an early current with a depolarized EGABA (-30.9 ± 1.6 mV; n = 9) and fast kinetics and a late current with a more hyperpolarized EGABA and slow kinetics (-44.5 ± 2.1 mV; n = 12).
This difference in EGABA for the fast and slow components is consistent with a gradient for the intracellular Cl- concentration between the somatic and dendritic compartments.
The kinetics of these GABAA-mediated PSCs is reminiscent of slow dendritic responses rather than fast perisomatic currents described in hippocampal neurons.
Spontaneous PSCs displayed a low frequency (0.05 ± 0.02 Hz; n = 6) and long rise and decay times.

source_title: GABA regulates synaptic integration of newly generated neurons in the adult brain
node_relevance_reason: Establishes the tonic and phasic GABA activation sequence in adult-born dentate granule cells with precise dpi chronology: tonic GABA precedes any phasic input (from 3 dpi), GABAergic PSCs first at 7 dpi, glutamatergic PSCs first at 14 dpi. Documents NKCC1-dependent high [Cl−]i as the mechanism for GABA depolarisation. Directly addresses the key electrophysiology gap in dg_neuroblast and dg_immature_granule_neuron nodes regarding GABA excitation.
summary: Ge et al. 2006 demonstrate in adult mice (C57Bl/6, 7-8 weeks) using whole-cell and gramicidin perforated patch-clamp recordings that newborn dentate granule cells receive tonic GABA activation from 3 dpi before any detectable synaptic input, followed by phasic GABAergic PSCs at 7 dpi and glutamatergic PSCs at 14 dpi. Newborn neurons express high NKCC1 and little KCC2, resulting in high intracellular [Cl−] and a depolarised EGABA that renders GABA excitatory rather than inhibitory. Knockdown of NKCC1 shifts EGABA negative, and NKCC1-knockdown neurons show significant defects in dendritic arborisation and synapse formation, demonstrating that GABA depolarisation is required for normal synaptic integration. No neuropeptides are reported.
quotes: At 3 days post viral injection (3 dpi), none of the GFP+ cells recorded under voltage-clamp (Vm= −65 mV) exhibited any spontaneous synaptic currents
tonic current in all GFP+ DGCs recorded from 3 dpi and onwards (n = 48)
Bicuculline (10 μM)-sensitive GABAergic PSCs...and CNQX (50 μM)-sensitive glutamatergic PSCs...were first detected in some GFP+ DGCs at 7 dpi and 14 dpi, respectively
newborn DGCs (DCX+) in the adult brain express high levels of NKCC1 and little KCC2
newborn neurons in the adult brain...follow a stereotypical integration process-receiving tonic GABA activation first, followed by GABAergic synaptic inputs and finally glutamatergic synaptic inputs
GABA exerts a depolarising action during the initial development of new DGCs
tonic GABA activation depolarises newborn DGCs...and constitutes the majority of GABA-induced activation
shRNA-NKCC1+ DGCs exhibited significant defects in their dendritic arborisation
The total dendritic length and branch number of these new neurons...were significantly reduced.

source_title: GABAergic excitation promotes neuronal differentiation in adult hippocampal progenitor cells
node_relevance_reason: Documents GABAergic depolarisation of type-2 neuronal progenitor cells (dg_neuroblast equivalent) in the SGZ using GFP-targeted patch-clamp recordings in nestin-GFP mice — directly addressing the electrophysiology gap for the dg_neuroblast node. Shows that GABA input to progenitors drives [Ca2+]i increase and NeuroD expression, linking electrical activity to neuronal fate commitment.
summary: Tozuka et al. 2005 used GFP-targeted recordings from type-2 neuronal progenitor cells in hippocampal slices from nestin-GFP mice and found that these cells receive direct GABAergic (not glutamatergic) neural inputs from the hippocampal circuitry. GABA depolarises type-2 progenitors due to their elevated [Cl−]i. This depolarisation initiates a [Ca2+]i increase and NeuroD expression. BrdU pulse labelling with GABAA-R agonists confirms that GABAergic excitation promotes activity-dependent neuronal differentiation. No neuropeptides are reported. Species: mouse (nestin-GFP transgenic).
quotes: neuronal progenitor (type-2) cells receive active direct neural inputs from the hippocampal circuitry
This input was GABAergic but not glutamatergic.
The GABAergic inputs depolarized type-2 cells because of their elevated [Cl(-)](i).
This excitation initiated an increase of [Ca(2+)](i) and the expression of NeuroD.
GABAergic inputs to hippocampal progenitor cells promote activity-dependent neuronal differentiation.

source_title: Functional neurogenesis in the adult hippocampus
node_relevance_reason: Landmark paper providing the first direct patch-clamp evidence that newly generated neurons in the adult mouse hippocampus fire action potentials, have functional synaptic inputs, and are electrophysiologically integrated by 4 weeks post-infection — directly filling the dg_immature_granule_neuron electrophysiology gap. Documents passive and active membrane properties of adult-born vs mature granule cells.
summary: van Praag et al. 2002 used retroviral GFP labelling in adult female C57Bl/6 mice and whole-cell patch-clamp recordings (4–8 weeks post-injection) to show that newly generated granule cells fire action potentials (Vth −45.8 mV, plateau ~140 Hz), have smaller membrane capacitance (42.3 pF vs 99.2 pF in mature cells) and shorter time constants (16.6 ms vs 33.7 ms), and receive spontaneous synaptic inputs (0.4–5 Hz) including perforant path-evoked responses. Resting membrane potential is slightly depolarised compared to mature cells (−69.7 vs −74.8 mV). These properties confirm functional neuronal integration into the hippocampal circuit by 4 weeks. No neuropeptides are reported.
quotes: newly generated cells in the adult mouse hippocampus have neuronal morphology and can display passive membrane properties, action potentials and functional synaptic inputs similar to those found in mature dentate granule cells
Action potentials displayed a threshold (Vth) of −43 mV, conspicuous after hyperpolarization and frequency adaptation
membrane potentials recorded in response to depolarizing current steps displayed repetitive spiking with increasing frequency, reaching a plateau at about 140 Hz
Membrane capacitance (pF): Newly generated 42.3 (10.2); Mature 99.2 (12.7); P=0.003
Time constant (ms): Newly generated 16.6 (4.0); Mature 33.7 (3.7); P=0.003
Resting potential (mV): Newly generated −69.7 (1.7); Mature −74.8 (1.1); P=0.06
Spiking threshold (mV): Newly generated −45.8 (2.9); Mature −39.6 (1.5); P=0.07
newly generated granule cells are functionally integrated into the circuitry by 4 weeks
Experiments on newly generated neurons (GFP+) were carried out 4–8 weeks after viral injection.

source_title: Distinct Morphological Stages of Dentate Granule Neuron Maturation in the Adult Mouse Hippocampus
node_relevance_reason: Provides functional context for the morphological stages: immature neurons have lower LTP threshold, and states that connectivity timing determines when neurons become functionally relevant — relevant to the electrophysiology gap in dg_immature_granule_neuron but does not provide original patch-clamp data.
summary: Zhao 2006 discusses the functional implications of the morphological maturation stages. The paper notes that immature neurons have a lower threshold for long-term potentiation induction, providing indirect evidence for the enhanced excitability of immature granule neurons. The morphological staging maps onto distinct periods of synaptogenesis, with stage C (16 dpi onwards) being when major synaptic connections are established. The study is entirely in adult mouse (mus musculus).
quotes: Immature neurons have also been shown to have a lower threshold for long-term potentiation induction.

source_title: PSA Depletion Induces the Differentiation of Immature Neurons in the Piriform Cortex of Adult Mice
node_relevance_reason: Provides data on PSA-NCAM role in neuronal immaturity and a functional electrophysiology observation (AIS and action potential firing capacity in mature vs immature neurons), applicable to dg_neuroblast and dg_immature_granule_neuron node gaps, but data is from piriform cortex rather than DG.
summary: Coviello 2021 examines PSA-NCAM depletion effects on immature neuron differentiation in piriform cortex. Using DCX-CreERT2 lineage tracing, cells are classified through 5 morphological maturity stages. A key functional observation is that AIS (axon initial segment) presence in mature neurons correlates with capacity for repetitive action potential firing, absent in immature precursors. PSA depletion promotes differentiation.
quotes: the presence of AIS on mature, complex cells of the PCX layer II provided the capacity of repetitive action potential firing that was absent in less mature precursors devoid of AIS

The number of GFP+ cells coexpressing markers of immature neurons decreased dramatically when the animals were sacrificed 6 months after tamoxifen administration (3m-t→9) (PSA-NCAM+ 4.7 ± 1.7%, P < 0.0001***; DCX+ 6.0 ± 1.4%, P < 0.0001***; DCX+/PSA-NCAM+ 3.25 ± 1%, P < 0.0001***)

source_title: Cellular Plasticity in the Adult Murine Piriform Cortex: Continuous Maturation of Dormant Precursors Into Excitatory Neurons
node_relevance_reason: Provides progressive DCX+/PSA-NCAM+ immature neuron marker dynamics and CaMKII (glutamatergic) marker acquisition data in piriform cortex. Relevant to dg_mature_granule_neuron glutamatergic identity and dg_neuroblast marker loss as maturation occurs, but not from DG tissue.
summary: Rotheneichner 2018 tracks piriform cortex immature neurons through maturation using DCX-CreERT2 lineage tracing. DCX and PSA-NCAM co-expression decreases dramatically by 6 months post-tamoxifen (DCX+ 6.0 ± 1.4%, PSA-NCAM+ 4.7 ± 1.7%). CaMKII (glutamatergic marker) expression increases progressively from 2.0% at 3m to 80.0% in mature neurons, demonstrating glutamatergic differentiation. Dendritic spine density increases from 0.31 to 0.88 spines/μm during maturation.
quotes: The proportion of GFP⁺CaMKII⁺ coexpressing cells increased significantly between the 3m-t group (2.0 ± 2.3%) and 9m-t group (12 ± 2.3%), reaching 80.0 ± 7.7% in mature neurons.

These observations are in accordance with a study performed in the piriform cortex by Klempin and colleagues (2011), who reported that cells with a low DCX expression and a complex morphology, reminiscent of immature semilunar–pyramidal transitional neurons, exhibited an electrophysiological profile consistent with neurons undergoing maturation

source_title: Widespread Doublecortin Expression in the Cerebral Cortex of the Octodon degus
node_relevance_reason: Characterizes DCX+ cells in DG as NeuN-negative (not yet mature) and proliferating (Ki67/PCNA+), relevant to dg_neuroblast negative markers (NeuN−) and confirming proliferative status of DCX+ cells in the SGZ. Cross-species comparison context.
summary: Groen 2021 examines DCX expression across brain regions in the Octodon degus. In the DG hippocampus, DCX labeling is dense in the SGL and some mossy fibers. A key finding is that DCX-labeled neurons in the DG are not fully mature (NeuN-negative), and some DG DCX cells are labeled by PCNA and Ki67 (proliferation markers). In neocortex, DCX+ neurons lack NeuN, confirming immaturity.
quotes: none of the DCX-labeled neurons in the hippocampus or neocortex was also labeled with either calretinin or NeuN, indicating the DCX-labeled neurons in the DG are not yet fully mature neurons.
In the hippocampus, some of the cells that are labeled by DCX are also labeled by PCNA and Ki67, two cell cycle markers

source_title: New GABAergic interneurons in the adult neocortex and striatum are generated from different precursors
node_relevance_reason: Provides relevant cross-regional comparison: new cortical neurons express NeuN and EAAC-1 (glutamate transporter), and adult-born DG granule cells undergo developmental switch from CR to CB expression (calretinin to calbindin), directly relevant to dg_immature_granule_neuron (Calretinin+, Calbindin-) vs dg_mature_granule_neuron (Calbindin+) marker distinction.
summary: Dayer 2005 describes new neuron generation in adult neocortex and compares to DG neurogenesis. A key cross-regional observation directly relevant to the DG node context: adult-born DG granule neurons make a developmental switch from calretinin to calbindin expression, analogous to what is seen in new cortical interneurons. This transition from calretinin+ (immature) to calbindin+ (mature) is explicitly discussed.
quotes: Calbindin (CB) and calretinin (CR) immunoreactivity were each observed in 4–5-wk-old and 11–12-wk-old BrdU+ cells. In triple-labeled sections, no BrdU+/CR+ cells appeared to be CB+, suggesting that there are two distinct classes of newly generated cortical interneurons or, alternatively, that new interneurons make a developmental switch from CR to CB expression like the adult-born dentate gyrus granule neurons.

Examination of brain sections from rats injected with BrdU 4–5 wk earlier revealed BrdU+ cells that were double labeled with antibodies against each of three neuronal markers: NeuN, a marker specific for mature neurons; EAAC-1, a neuronal glutamate transporter; and HuC/D, neuron-specific RNA binding proteins.

source_title: Postnatal and Adult Neurogenesis in Mammals, Including Marsupials
node_relevance_reason: Confirms glutamatergic identity of DG granule cells and describes migration from SGZ to GCL, which is relevant to dg_mature_granule_neuron node. However, the review lacks detailed stage-specific marker information or electrophysiology.
summary: Bartkowska 2022 reviews adult neurogenesis across mammals including marsupials. It confirms that DG granule cells are glutamatergic (in contrast to olfactory bulb granule cells which are GABAergic) and describes the migration of new neurons from SGZ to GCL. The paper states that neurogenesis in the DG begins at E12.5 in mice and decreases at P14.
quotes: Granule cells in the DG are glutaminergic, while granule cells of the OB are GABAergic.
new neurons generated in the DG migrate a short distance from the subgranular layer to the granular layer of the DG and remain within this structure

source_title: Mimicking Neural Stem Cell Niche by Biocompatible Substrates
section: 2. Embryonic and Adult Neural Stem Cells
node_relevance_reason: Provides concise description of DG neurogenesis stages and timeline: radial type-I and nonradial type-II precursors → intermediate progenitors → neuroblasts → immature neurons (inner GCL) → differentiated granule cells extending dendrites and mossy fiber axons. Relevant to dg_neuroblast and dg_immature_granule_neuron node morphology fields.
summary: Regalado-Santiago 2016 reviews neural stem cell biology and includes a standard description of DG neurogenesis stages. New neurons extend dendrites toward the molecular layer and project axons through the hilus toward CA3 within days of reaching the inner GCL. The paper focuses on biomimetic substrates for neural stem cell culture.
quotes: hippocampal neurogenesis in the DG begins, as in SVZ neurogenesis, with the proliferation of radial (type I cell) and nonradial (type II cell) precursors that give rise to intermediate progenitors, which in turn generate neuroblasts.

immature neurons move into the inner granule cell layer and differentiate into dentate granule cells in the hippocampus. Within days, newborn neurons extend dendrites toward the molecular layer and project axons through the hilus toward the CA3.

source_title: Prenatal genesis of layer II doublecortin expressing neurons in neonatal and young adult guinea pig cerebral cortex
node_relevance_reason: Describes DCX+ cells in DG SGZ morphologically but focuses on neocortical layer II DCX+ neurons in guinea pig — not DG-specific. Mentions electrophysiological properties as a known feature of DCX+ immature neurons but provides no original electrophysiology data.
summary: Yang 2015 describes DCX+ cell distributions in neonatal and young adult guinea pig cortex and DG. A large amount of DCX+ cells in the SGZ of the DG are noted with dendritic processes extending toward the molecular layer. The paper birth-dates and characterizes cortical DCX+ cells as a unique population with slow differentiation pace, but lacks electrophysiological data for DG-specific cells.
quotes: A large amount of DCX+ cells was present at the subgranular zone (SGZ) of the dentate gyrus with their dendritic processes extending across the granule cell layer (GCL) toward the molecular layer (ML).

source_title: Commentary: Posttraining ablation of adult-generated olfactory granule cells degrades odor-reward memories
node_relevance_reason: Addresses adult neurogenesis and functional integration of adult-born neurons, but context is olfactory bulb granule cells (GABAergic), not DG granule cells (glutamatergic). Mentions synaptic integration timing at 21 days for OB neurons.
summary: Vangeneugden 2015 is a commentary on adult olfactory bulb granule cell function. It notes that adult-born OGCs become synaptically integrated and that ablation of 21-day-old (but not 10-day-old) neurons degrades memory, suggesting functional integration requires this maturation period. Not directly relevant to DG neuroblast electrophysiology.
quotes: Degraded memories only occurred following ablation of synaptically integrated 21 days-old, but not immature 10 days-old neurons.
During this critical period, adult-born OGCs exhibit heightened plasticity at input synapses from cortical regions.

source_title: Protective Effect of Antioxidants on Neuronal Dysfunction and Plasticity in Huntington's Disease
section: 4. Functional Significance and Regulation of Adult Neurogenesis
node_relevance_reason: Contains basic description of DG neurogenesis (SGZ proliferation → IPC → immature neurons → inner GCL → granule cells → mossy fiber projections), but this is contextual background in a Huntington's disease review with no DG-specific electrophysiology or marker data.
summary: Velusamy 2017 reviews antioxidants in Huntington's disease, briefly describing adult hippocampal neurogenesis. New neurons from NSCs develop into intermediate progenitors, then neuroblasts, then immature neurons that migrate into the inner GCL, extend dendrites toward the molecular layer and project axons as mossy fibers in the hilus region.
quotes: In the hippocampal SGZ, proliferating NSCs develop into intermediate progenitors, which generate neuroblasts or immature neurons. These newly generated immature neurons migrate into the inner granule cell layer (GCL) and differentiate into new granule neurons of the hippocampus. Further, these newborn neurons extend dendrites from DG towards the molecular layer (ML) and project axons that form the mossy fibber tract in the hilus region.

source_title: Ameliorating effect of postweaning exposure to antioxidant on disruption of hippocampal neurogenesis induced by developmental hypothyroidism in rats.
node_relevance_reason: No PMC ID available for this paper; could not fetch fulltext. Study concerns antioxidant effects on hypothyroidism-disrupted hippocampal neurogenesis in rats — tangentially related to DG neurogenesis markers but not directly relevant to the electrophysiology, marker, or classification gaps in the node context.
summary: No fulltext available via Europe PMC (no PMC ID). This paper by Tanaka 2019 examines antioxidant effects on disrupted hippocampal neurogenesis caused by developmental hypothyroidism in rats. It likely contains rodent DG marker data (DCX, NeuN) but the specific content could not be retrieved.
